article.title: PostgreSQL Ships Quarterly Update for Versions 14 Through 18, Patching 11 Security Vulnerabilities and Over 60 Bugs
article.summary: The May 14 update across PostgreSQL 14-18 fixes 11 CVEs, including an integer-overflow memory flaw and SQL injection in replication tools.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The PostgreSQL Global Development Group released a coordinated update to every supported version of the database on May 14, 2026, shipping PostgreSQL 18.4, 17.10, 16.14, 15.18, and 14.23. According to the [PostgreSQL Global Development Group](https://www.postgresql.org/about/news/postgresql-184-1710-1614-1518-and-1423-released-3297/), the release "fixes 11 security vulnerabilities and over 60 bugs reported over the last several months." It is the project's regular second-quarter maintenance release rather than an out-of-cycle emergency patch.

## What We Know

The update addresses 11 distinct CVEs. The project lists the affected range as [supported versions 14 through 18](https://www.postgresql.org/about/news/postgresql-184-1710-1614-1518-and-1423-released-3297/), with two of the issues scoped to narrower version ranges.

The most severe memory-safety issue is CVE-2026-6473, described in the [PostgreSQL 18.4 release notes](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/release-18-4.html) as a fix for "assorted integer overflows in memory-allocation calculations," credited to Tom Lane, Nathan Bossart, and Heikki Linnakangas. The notes state that overflow "would lead to allocating a too-small buffer which the caller would then write past the end of," which "would at least trigger server crashes, and probably could be exploited for arbitrary code execution," though in many cases the hazard exists only in 32-bit builds. [Linux Compatible](https://www.linuxcompatible.org/story/postgresql-184-1710-1614-1518-and-1423-released) characterized the same flaw as "particularly nasty because it lets crafted application inputs trigger out of bounds writes that crash the backend process."

Two of the patched issues are SQL injection vulnerabilities in PostgreSQL's logical replication tooling. Per the [release notes](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/release-18-4.html), CVE-2026-6476 stems from `pg_createsubscriber` inserting a subscription name into SQL commands without quoting, so that "SQL injection could be achieved" when the name comes from an untrusted source. CVE-2026-6638, credited to Pavel Kohout, covers `ALTER SUBSCRIPTION ... REFRESH PUBLICATION` interpolating schema and relation names into SQL commands without quoting them, "allowing execution of arbitrary SQL on the publisher." A third injection issue, CVE-2026-6637, fixes SQL injection and buffer overruns in the `contrib/spi` example module.

Other fixes in the batch, all detailed in the [release notes](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/release-18-4.html), include CVE-2026-6479, which prevents "unbounded recursion while processing startup packets" that a malicious client could use to crash a backend; CVE-2026-6475, which prevents path traversal in `pg_basebackup` and `pg_rewind` after those tools "failed to validate output file paths read from their input"; and CVE-2026-6478, which switches authentication code to timing-safe string comparisons when checking passwords and hashes. [Linux Compatible](https://www.linuxcompatible.org/story/postgresql-184-1710-1614-1518-and-1423-released) summarized the overall set as "memory corruption bugs, SQL injection vectors in replication tools, and a sneaky timing leak that could expose MD5 passwords."

Beyond the security content, the [release notes](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/release-18-4.html) document non-security bug fixes spanning query planning, replication, and crash recovery — among them a correction so the `MERGE` statement "correctly reports serialization failures in repeatable-read/serializable modes," a fix for an integer overflow in window-function frame position calculations, and a fix for a "race condition between checkpoint replay and multixact ID creations" during WAL replay.

Applying the update does not require a data migration. The [PostgreSQL Global Development Group](https://www.postgresql.org/about/news/postgresql-184-1710-1614-1518-and-1423-released-3297/) states that "users are not required to dump and reload their database or use `pg_upgrade` in order to apply this update release; you may simply stop PostgreSQL and update its binaries." [Linux Compatible](https://www.linuxcompatible.org/story/postgresql-184-1710-1614-1518-and-1423-released) notes that minor updates are fully cumulative, so administrators on an older minor release pick up every intervening fix at once.

The release also carries an end-of-life reminder. The [PostgreSQL Global Development Group](https://www.postgresql.org/about/news/postgresql-184-1710-1614-1518-and-1423-released-3297/) states that "PostgreSQL 14 will stop receiving fixes on November 12, 2026," and recommends that production users of that version plan an upgrade to a newer, supported release.

## What We Don't Know

The announcement and release notes do not assign CVSS severity scores or indicate whether any of the 11 vulnerabilities have been exploited in the wild. Several of the SQL injection issues are gated on an untrusted input source — for example, an attacker-controlled subscription name — and the project does not estimate how common those configurations are in practice. The exact distribution of the more than 60 non-security bug fixes across the five released versions is also not enumerated in the announcement.

## Analysis

The May release is notable less for any single headline flaw than for how the fixes cluster around PostgreSQL's logical replication machinery: `pg_createsubscriber`, `ALTER SUBSCRIPTION ... REFRESH PUBLICATION`, and the replication-adjacent `contrib/spi` module all received SQL injection hardening in the same batch, per the [release notes](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/release-18-4.html). Logical replication has become a standard building block for upgrades and cross-region setups, which widens the surface where an untrusted name can reach an unquoted SQL string. The integer-overflow fix (CVE-2026-6473) is the one with the broadest theoretical impact given the release notes' mention of possible arbitrary code execution, but the project's own framing — that the hazard is mostly confined to 32-bit builds — tempers that for the majority of modern 64-bit deployments. With PostgreSQL 14's support window now closing on November 12, 2026, this release doubles as a prompt for the remaining 14.x installations to begin planning a move.
